bibliographic_data.title: Modeling Daily Seasonality of Mexico City Ozone using Nonseparable Covariance Models on Circles Cross Time
bibliographic_data.abstract: Mexico City tracks ground-level ozone levels to assess compliance with national ambient air quality standards and prevent environmental health emergencies. Ozone levels show distinct daily patterns, within the city, and over the course of the year. To model these data, we use covariance models over space, circular time, and linear time. We review existing models and develop new classes of nonseparable covariance models appropriate for quasi-periodic data collected at many locations. With these covariance models, we use nearest-neighbor Gaussian processes to predict hourly ozone levels at unobserved locations in April and May, the peak ozone season, to infer compliance to Mexican air quality standards and estimate respiratory health risk associated with ozone.

core_content.novelty_claim: Develops new classes of nonseparable covariance models for quasi-periodic data on circles cross time to predict hourly ozone levels in Mexico City.
core_content.key_methodology: Uses nearest-neighbor Gaussian processes with nonseparable covariance models over space, circular time, and linear time to predict hourly ozone levels at unobserved locations.
outcomes: Developed new classes of nonseparable covariance functions for circles cross time that improve predictive performance for Mexico City data.
Predicted compliance with air quality standards and estimated respiratory health risk in April and May, the peak ozone season.
core_content.full_text_markdown: This paper addresses the modeling of daily seasonal patterns of ground-level ozone levels in Mexico City. The authors use covariance models over space, circular time, and linear time to predict hourly ozone levels at unobserved locations during the peak ozone season (April and May). They develop new nonseparable covariance functions for circles cross time that improve predictive performance compared to existing alternatives. Using nearest-neighbor Gaussian processes with these models, they assess compliance with national air quality standards and estimate respiratory health risks associated with ozone exposure.
